reasoning: The US national average savings rate (SNDR) has been stagnant at 0.40% for the last three months (Sept-Nov 2025) and has generally trended downward from 0.43% a year ago. According to Federal Reserve statements and market expectations from late October and early November 2025, the central bank is in a rate-cutting cycle, with the median FOMC participant projecting the federal funds rate to drop to the 3.50%-3.75% range by the end of 2025 (search_0_result_2, search_0_result_9). Savings account rates are historically sticky but tend to follow the downward trend of benchmark rates. There is little incentive for banks to increase savings rates while the Fed is cutting or holding. The statistical trend analysis from the FRED tool estimates a 30% probability of the rate exceeding 0.40% by December 2025, but given the specific 'more cuts' signal from the FOMC, this is likely an overestimate. As the forecast horizon extends to February 2026, uncertainty increases, though the overall environment remains unfavorable for a rate increase compared to the November 2025 baseline.
